title: Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety
summary: Rules for heat, light, cooking, ventilation, and carbon monoxide prevention when electricity is out.
content: During blackouts people often create the danger themselves by bringing outdoor heat sources indoors, blocking ventilation, using candles near fabric, or sleeping near open flame. Carbon monoxide has no reliable warning smell and can kill quietly. Treat every fuel-burning device as dangerous indoors unless it is designed, installed, maintained, and vented for indoor use. Prefer flashlights over candles. Keep exits clear. Put heat and cooking tasks on a schedule so tired people are not improvising at night. If anyone develops headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, weakness, or unusual sleepiness near a burning device, move to fresh air and seek emergency help.
steps: Ban outdoor stoves, grills, charcoal, and generators from indoor spaces.
Keep candles away from curtains, beds, paper, plastic, children, and pets.
Use one stable lighting area instead of many open flames.
Keep exits and stairs clear before dark.
Assign a person to check that flames and heat sources are off before sleep.
Ventilate safely without exposing people to cold or smoke.
warnings: Never run a generator indoors, in a garage, in a stairwell, or near windows.
Never sleep with an open flame burning.
Carbon monoxide can be present without smoke.
common_mistakes: Closing every air gap around a burning device.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety.
short_term: For the first hours of Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Blackout fire and carbon monoxide safety to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
